name: The Greeks and Hedging
tagline: Five Greeks measure five risks: Delta (price), Gamma (curvature), Theta (time), Vega (vol), Rho (rate). Delta-hedging neutralizes the dominant risk; gamma and vega govern second-order effects.

blocks.text: The Greeks are partial derivatives of an option price with respect to its inputs. Delta = dV/dS measures sensitivity to the stock; Gamma = d^2V/dS^2 measures convexity (rate of delta change); Theta = dV/dt is time decay; Vega = dV/dsigma is volatility sensitivity; Rho = dV/dr is rate sensitivity. For European calls: Delta = exp(-yT) * N(d_1); Gamma = exp(-yT) * N'(d_1) / (S * sigma * sqrt(T)); Theta and Vega have closed forms involving N'(d_1).

Delta-hedging neutralizes the first-order risk: hold Delta shares short for each long call (or vice versa). Beyond delta, the BSM PDE Theta + (1/2)*sigma^2*S^2*Gamma = r*V says that gamma and theta TRADE OFF for delta-hedged positions: positive gamma (convexity benefit) is offset by negative theta (time decay). The 'gamma scalper' bets on realized vol exceeding implied; the 'theta seller' bets the other way.

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: Delta is the slope; Gamma is the curvature. Delta-hedging captures slope; you're then exposed to gamma (positive = convex profit if S moves either way) and theta (negative = pay time decay). The PDE forces these into balance under risk-neutral pricing.

whenToUse: Setting up a delta-hedge for a long or short option position.
Pricing approximation for ATM options: c ~ 0.4 * sigma * S * sqrt(T).
Reasoning about second-order risks (gamma scalping, vega exposure).
Understanding why deep-ITM American puts can have positive theta and merit early exercise.
Recovering implied volatility from market prices (vega is the slope of c with respect to sigma).
recipe: Write the option price V(S, t; sigma, r) and identify the Greek as a partial derivative.
For Delta of a call: N(d_1) (multiplied by exp(-yT) with dividends).
For Gamma: same N'(d_1) / (S * sigma * sqrt(T)).
To delta-hedge: short Delta shares (for a long call); rebalance as S moves.
commonPitfalls: Treating N(d_1) and N(d_2) as constants when differentiating -- they're functions of S.
Forgetting the identity S * N'(d_1) = K * exp(-rT) * N'(d_2), which makes Delta = N(d_1) clean.
Confusing Delta hedging (first-order) with full replication (which also requires dynamic re-hedging).
Assuming Theta is always negative -- deep-ITM European puts can have positive Theta.
Setting up the hedge based on REAL-WORLD probabilities rather than risk-neutral probabilities.
